constitution_day.intro: Norway's National Day on 17 May, Grunnlovsdagen, celebrates the 1814 constitution with children's parades and a sea of flags.
constitution_day.history: On 17 May 1814 Norway signed its constitution at Eidsvoll, declaring independence as Denmark ceded the country to Sweden. Though soon forced into union with Sweden, Norwegians kept the day; full independence came in 1905. The charter is among the world’s oldest still in use.
constitution_day.traditions: Rather than military parades, Norway fills its streets with children’s processions (barnetog) waving flags, marching bands and people in traditional ‚bunad‘ costume. Families eat ice cream and hot dogs, and the royal family greets the Oslo parade from the palace balcony.
funFacts: Norway celebrates with children's parades, not military ones.
Many Norwegians wear the traditional regional 'bunad' costume.
Norway's 1814 constitution is among the oldest still in force.
